 ONE SENTEN CE SUMMARY 30 
Patient -derived immunocompetent cultures uncover responses to immunotherapy combinations to guide 31 
precision oncology in ovarian cancer. 32 
 33 
SHORT TITLE 34 
iPDCs for precision immuno -oncology in ovarian cancer . 35

3 
 ABSTRACT 36 
Identifying effective immunotherapies requires testing in pre -clinical models that represent the patient -specific 37 
immune microenvironment. Here, we established a unique patient -derived immunocompetent culture (iPDC) 38 
model for high -grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) cultured on a novel physiologically relevant patient - 39 
derived matrix. We validate our fu nctional immuno -oncology platform using histological, genomic and single - 40 
cell analysis, and show that the iPDC responses significantly correlate with the patient’s clinical responses and 41 
progression free survival. We identify new therapeutic options associ ated with distinct tumor cells states in 42 
relapsed, therapy resistant HGSC patients. Using h igh-throughput drug testing followed by single cell -imaging, 43 
we discover ataxia telangiectasia and Rad3 -related inhibitor (ATRi) combined with a novel immunotherapy 44 
targeting Autotaxin (ATX) as a promising new combination treatment for HGSC. The iPDC responses to ATRi 45 
plus ATX inhibitor associate with immune cell activation and a distinct source tumor biomarker profile. In 46 
conclusion, iPDCs serve as a unique, high-throughput and functional platform for testing immunotherapy 47 
combinations and predictive biomarker discovery for precision oncology in HGSC 48

4 
 INTRODUCTION 49 
High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma is the most common subtype of ovarian cancer, often diagnosed at an 50 
advanced stage with 10 -year survival of 15% (1). Nearly 80% of the HGSC metastasize to omentum, a large 51 
fat tissue covering stomach and intestine, which provides metabolic and inflammatory microenvironment for 52 
cancer and immune cells (2). The s tandard of care for HGSC includes primary debulking surgery followed by 53 
platinum -based chemotherapy . Despite the successful response to initial treatment, recurrent disease often 54 
develops within the first three years . Poly (ADP -ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPi) have improved patient’s 55 
outcome when used as a maintenance treatment after first -line chemotherapy, particularly in patients with 56 
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), and or sensitivity to platinum -based chemotherapy (3). 57 
However , 35-45% of the patients discontinue the treatment due to innate or acquired resistance to PARPi (4, 58 
5), suggesting that novel combination strategies are required to overcome resistance to PARPi . 59 
Single agent immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have shown limited efficacy in HGSC with overall response 60 
rate of 10% (6). The l ack of success with ICIs in HGSC is likely due to a variety of immunosuppressive cells 61 
and mechanisms in the tumor microenvironment (TME) (7).

niraparib and anti -PD1 antibody, pembrolizumab (12). In addition, BRCA 1/2 defective HGSC has been shown 70

cell death (PD -1) and its ligand (PD -L1) compared to HRP tumors (13-15). These findings highlight the 72

 immunotherapies, the efficacy of these agents needs to be validated in immunocompetent models representing 78 
patient tumor -specific immune microenvironment . 79 
A variety of patient -derived ex vivo models have been developed to model tumor immune microenvironment, 80 
and to evaluate response to immunotherapies (18-20). All these studies have utilized matrigel or collagen - 81 
based matrices to culture the cells or tissue fragments. However, the source of these matrices being commonly 82 
of mouse or rat origin, do not represent HGSC patient TME. In addition, previous studies using patient -derived 83 
models have primarily focused on the e ffect of ICIs on functional responses from immune cells but not the 84 
cytotoxic response from tumor cells. Furthermore, these models are not suitable for high -throughput 85 
combinatorial drug testing. Human tissue -specific extracellular matri ces (ECM) have been produced to model 86 
the primary and metastatic TME (21, 22). Towards this, Neilson et al., have generated and characterized a 87 
human omentum gel matrix (OmGel) from tumor -free omentum , which has the potential utility for studying 88 
omental metastatic microenvironment across a plethora of tumor types with frequent metastasis to oment um 89 
(23). Here we established a functional precision immuno -oncology platform using novel HGSC patient -derived 90

a plethora of tumor types with frequent metastasis to oment um 89 
(23). Here we established a functional precision immuno -oncology platform using novel HGSC patient -derived 90 
immunocompetent cultures (iPDCs) using patient -derived OmGel matrix . We validated that the iPDCs 91 
preserve tumor and immune cell features of the native tumor and recapitulate the clinical responses to 92 
chemotherapy and PARPis. Importantly, by coupling single cell image -based drug responses with immune cell 93 
functional states we show that iPDCs reveal patient -specific vulnerabilities to novel immuno -oncology and 94 
combination therap ies, which associate with distinct tissue biomarkers in HGSC. 95

 Tumor -cell pRPA presents as a potential biomarker for ATRi plus ATXi combi nation treatment. 272 
Increase in replication stress markers such as phosphorylated replication stress protein A (RPA) has been 273 
suggested to be a predictive biomarker for sensitivity to ATRi (35). To evaluate if this is also reflected in the 274 
patient’s tumors, we performed tissue cyclic multiplex immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) (36) using antibodies 275 
against PAX8 (tumor cells), vimentin and replication stress marker pRPA32 -RPA2(Ser8) on the corresponding 276

analysis of the tumor sa mples revealed that the majority of iPDCs responding the ATRi combinations were 278 
characterized by higher levels of pRPA32 in the tumor cells, as compared to the non -responding tumors ( Fig. 279 
4, E-F). Together, these results confirm the patient responses to co mbination of ATRi and immunotherapies 280 
and highlight the potential of our iPDC platform in screening of novel combinatorial treatments combined with 281

 DISCUSSION 283 
Here we established a novel patient -derived immunocompetent culture platform of HGSC tumors, and validate 284 
it using histologigy, genomic and single -cell analysis. Importantly, our results highlight that iPDCs mimic the 285 
clinical responses to PARPi , and chemotherapy treatments thus validating the utility of iPDCs for testing 286 
chemotherapy and targeted therapies. In line with the clinical responses to PARPi, we found increased tumor 287 
cell sensitivity of HRD -iPDCs to PARPi olaparib compared to HRP -iPDCs . Importantly, the in vitro iPDC 288 
response to PARPi significantly correlated with matched patient’s clinical responses particularly, PFS and the 289 
duration of PARPi treatment in the clinics. These results validate the utility of iPDCs for testing therapeutic 290 
agents from primary and therapy -resistant tumors. 291 
Tissue -specific matrices have been shown to improve the prediction of treatment responses by preserving the 292 
native ECM cues in pancreatic cancer and lymphoid metastases (21, 22 ). Previous studies on HGSC organoid 293 
cultures have mostly utilized mouse sarcoma derived BME matrix (37). Our result demonstrate that the unique 294 
OmGel provides an optimal growth environment recapitulating th e HGSC metastatic tumors. The proteomic 295

analysis of OmGel by Neilson et al., report that there are no detectable levels of cytokines and growth factors 296 
in OmGel, suggesting that OmGel on its own does not alter the immune compartment of the iPDCs (23). In 297 
line with this we show that neither dispase digestion nor OmGel matrix alter the immune cells indicating that 298 
all the immune cells from the source tumors are faithfully recapitulated in the iPDCs, thereby making them an 299 
ideal model for the investigation of immunotherapy agents. As omentum is the most frequent site of 300 
intrabdominal metastases (38) our model with OmGel will be highly relevant for a broad range of 301 
gastrointestinal and gynecological cancers frequently metastasizing to the omentum. 302 
Thus far, high -throughput drug response profiling in HGSC has been performed predominantly on cancer cell 303 
cultures or organoi ds using compounds targeting oncogenic drivers. Herein we have established a patient - 304 
derived pre -clinical model and analysis pipelines that facilitate the investigation of immuno -oncology 305 
combinations in a high -throughput manner enabling the analysis of tr eatment responses to over 50 different 306

conditions on tumors from multiple patients. Moreover, while previous studies have mainly involved 307 
measurement of metabolic markers independent of the cell types, our single -cell image -based readout 308 
uniquely captures tumor and immune cell specific functional states. Although the evidence from clinical trials 309 
indicates unfavorable responses to ICIs in HGSC, these studies were conducted on unstratified patient 310

Although combination of ATRi with other DNA damaging agents such as PARPi, or WEE1i have been clinically 314 
tested in ovarian cancer (40, 41 ), the combination of ATRi with immunotherapy has not been investigated 315 
before. For the first time, we report that combination of ATRi with both investigational (ATXi, and PERKi), and 316 
established (anti -PD1 antibody) immunotherapies show patient -specific effects on tumor and immune cell 317 
viability. Furthermore, we found that higher concentrations (1µM or above) of ATRi are likely toxic as they 318 
caused both cancer and immune cell death in a subset of iPDCs. However, our results using lower 319 
concentrations of ATRi (0.1µM) in combination w ith ATXi showed cancer cell -specific death without affecting 320 
the immune cell viability in a tumor -dependent manner. Importantly, tumor cell -death responses to this 321 
combination aligned with CD8+ T cell and dendritic cell activation. Together our results hig hlight ATRi plus 322 
immunotherapies as a promising new combination treatment and suggest that iPDCs are suitable for screening 323

Our iPDC platform also enables identification of potential biomarkers for response and resistance to 325 
treatments. Combining the drug response from treatment resistant iPDCs with pathway analysis from scRNA - 326 
seq and mutation analysis from WES suggested possible mechanism of resistance to P ARPi. These include 327 
enrichment of cell cycle, G2M checkpoint, WNT, and TGFb pathways in PARPi resistant tumor, an acquired 328 
BRCA1 mutation likely leading to restoration of HR function. These findings support the concept of integrating 329 
drug sensitivity and r esistance profiling of iPDCs with molecular features from the corresponding tumors to 330 
reveal mechanism behind treatment responses to enable implementation of more effective treatment 331 
strategies. Furthermore, the results from t -CycIF corroborate previous st udies showing replication stress as a 332 
biomarker of sensitivity to ATRi and suggest that this approach aid in the investigation tissue -based biomarkers 333 
for potential combination therapies identified on iPDCs.

 The limitations of iPDCs include that they are short -term cultures lasting up to one week without the ability to 340 
passage over time. This is inherent to any relevant immuno -oncology ex vivo models that can sustain the 341 
viability and function al states of the diverse immune cell repertoire. Secondly, this platform only allows 342 
investigation of immunotherapy effects on intra -tumoral immune cells but not on the mobility and priming of 343 
the peripheral blood immune cells. Thirdly, the number of PARPi and resistant patients in our study is limited, 344 
due to the fact that these samples are difficult to get, and thus further validations using a larger number of 345 
recurrent samples are warranted. Despite these limitations, the fact that iPDCs harbor patient -matched 346 
intratumor immune cells makes them a useful preclinical model to evaluate effectiveness of immuno -oncology 347 
agents in activating intra -tumoral immune cells. Furthermore, our work highlight ATRi plus immunotherapy 348 
combinations as pote ntially effective agents in HGSC and demonstrate that coupling of iPDC drug responses 349 
with molecular and tissue -based features enable the discovery of predictive biomarkers. 350

SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIALS AND METHODS 1 
WES data processing and analysis 2 
Bioinformatic processing of DNA samples involved several steps, including quality control, alignment to 3 
the reference human genome, deduplication, cross -sample contamination estimation, and variant 4 
discovery. Sequenced DNA reads underwent quality control a nd trimming steps using FastQC (47) and 5 
Trimmomatic tools (48). Subsequently, the high -quality reads were aligned to the reference human 6 
genome GRCh38.d1.vd1 using BWA -MEM (49) with default parameters, subjected to deduplication with 7 
Picard tool and base quality recalibration using the Genome Analysis Toolkit (GATK) version 4.1.9.0 8 
(50). Cross -sample contamination estimation was conducted using GATK 4.1.9.0, with a contamination 9 
estimation threshold established at 15%. 10 
 11 
Mutation calling 12 
Somatic short variants were called through the collective analysis of multiple tumor samples against a 13 
singular matched normal for each patient, using GATK 4.1.4.1 Mutect2 according to established best 14 
practices. We used a Panel of Normal (PoN) generated with 181 normal samples from the prospective, 15 
longitudinal, multiregion observational study DECIDER (Multi -Layer Data to Improve Diagnosis, Predict 16 
Therapy Resistance and Suggest Targeted Therapies in HGSOC; ClinicalTrials.gov identifier 17

gion observational study DECIDER (Multi -Layer Data to Improve Diagnosis, Predict 16 
Therapy Resistance and Suggest Targeted Therapies in HGSOC; ClinicalTrials.gov identifier 17 
NCT04846933) and 99 TCGA normals. A detailed description of PoN creation can be found in Lahtinen 18 
et al (51). Subsequently, GATK FilterMutectCalls was used for variant filtration, keeping only those 19 
variants that successfully passing all filters. Variant allele frequencies (VAF) were computed based on 20 
the read depths for reference and alternat e alleles (AD field). We annotated the using ANNOVAR 21 
20191024 (52). 22 
 23 
iPDC -tissue concordance evaluation 24 
To assess the alignment between iPDC and tissue samples, we calculated tissue -based concordance 25 
(Ct) by determining the ratio of shared mutations in both iPDC (i) and tissue samples (t) to mutations 26 
found in the tissue (t) Ct = (Mi ∩ Mt) / Mt. Concordance analysis was conducted for iPDC -tissue pairs, 27 
with each pair consisting of iPDC and tissue samples derived from the same patient and organ. In total, 28 
10 iPDC -tissue pairs were analyzed. 29 
 30

iPDC -tissue VAF correlation 31 
Like iPDC -tissue concordance, we computed organoid -tissue Variant Allele Frequency (VAF) 32 
correlation for 10 pairs composed of iPDC and tissue samples from the same patient. 33 
 34 
Mutation and pathway analysis of diagnostic and resistant samples 35 
Exonic mutations were co llected from both primary and relapse samples for patients C865 and C429 . 36 
The mutated genes identified from these samples were then subjected to over -representation analysis 37 
using the ConsensusPathDB algorithm (5 3). 38
